narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 54.8% chance that you would fail it. This prediction draws from your academic record, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, family background, and other details like planning to attend full-time. As a Black male from a middle-income family, your profile shows strengths in some areas but key concerns that tipped the scales toward failure. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class (average decile of about 5.5). This low performance in your later undergraduate years stood out as the strongest signal of potential struggles ahead. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 36 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the average of 36.7 and shows solid aptitude. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 also helped somewhat, nudging things positively compared to the average of 3.21, though not enough to offset the top concern. Overall, while your full-time enrollment plan aligns with most students (over 90% of whom go this route), the dominant weight of that low Year 3 decile outweighed these positives, making your risk profile higher than typical admitted applicants who tend to have stronger late-stage grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building your upper-year record if possible, boosting your LSAT further, or exploring post-bac programs to demonstrate improvement. Law schools value resilience, so documenting any steps you've taken since these metrics could strengthen future applications. You have the foundation to turn this around with focused effort.
